title: Accepting contributions
description: Understand the rules for what contributions you can accept as trustee of a self-managed super fund (SMSF).
keywords: SMSF contributions, self managed super fund contributions, acceptable contribution, accepting contributions, allowable contribution, non-mandated contributions, mandated contributions in specie, SMSF contribution caps

content_blocks.text: Contributions you can accept
As a self-managed super fund (SMSF) trustee, you can only accept allowable contributions. For a contribution to be allowable:

items: it must be an accepted type of contribution, such as mandated employer contributions other contributions in-specie contributions
the member must meet age restrictions
member contributions can only be accepted if the SMSF also has the member's tax file number (TFN) .

content_blocks.text: When you accept a contribution
When you accept a contribution you must:

items: properly document the contribution, including the amount, type and breakdown of components
allocate it to the member’s account within 28 days of the end of the month in which you received them
accurately report contributions made to your fund during the financial year in your SMSF annual return.

content_blocks.text: Member's tax file number
When a member joins your fund, you should ask for their TFN and give it to us. You can do this when you register the fund or when a new member joins.
A member is not required by law to give their TFN. However, if they don't:

items: your fund can't accept member contributions for them, such as personal and eligible spouse contributions
your fund will have to pay additional tax on their mandated employer contributions
there may be administrative delays if we can't identify the member from the other information you provided
the member may not be able to receive super co-contributions .

content_blocks.text: If the member hasn’t provided their TFN and you’ve accepted member contributions for them, you’ll need to return the contribution within 30 days of becoming aware of the contribution. However, if they provide their TFN within 30 days of receiving the contribution, you don’t have to return the amount.

If you receive employer contributions on behalf of a member and you pay additional income tax because you did not have your member’s TFN, you may be able to claim a tax offset in a later financial year if the member later gives you their TFN.
Mandated employer contributions
Mandated employer contributions are contributions made by an employer under a law or industrial agreement for the benefit of a fund member. They include super guarantee contributions.
You can accept mandated employer contributions for members at any time, regardless of their age or the number of hours they’re working.
If your SMSF receives contributions from employers (other than related-party employers), your SMSF will need an electronic service address (ESA).
Other contributions
Other contributions are not required by law as well as Government contributions. They include:

items: contributions made by employers over and above their super guarantee or award obligations (such as salary sacrifice contributions)

member contributions – these are contributions made by or on behalf of a member, such as personal contributions salary sacrifice contributions eligible proceeds from main residence disposal ( downsizer super contributions ) government super co-contributions paid parental leave superannuation contributions eligible spouse contributions contributions made by a third party, such as an insurer
re-contributing COVID-19 early release super withdrawals .

content_blocks.text: When you can accept other contributions
The contributions you are able to accept are affected by the member's age.
Members under 75 years old
The rules for members aged under 75 years old have changed over time.
From 1 July 2022 , you can accept all types of other contributions. This includes downsizer super contributions where the member has reached eligible age for the downsizer scheme .
For a member turning 75 years old, contributions must be received no later than 28 days after the end of the month they turn 75.

Between 1 July 2020 and 30 June 2022 , you could accept all types of other contributions for members under 67 years old. If the member was between 67 and 75 years old, they needed to satisfy the work test or the work test exemption before you could accept other contributions.

To satisfy the work test the member must have been gainfully employed for at least 40 hours during a consecutive 30-day period. Where members don't meet the work test, other contributions can be accepted for an extra 12 months where:

items: they satisfied the work test in the financial year before the contribution was made
their total super balance was less than $300,000 at the end of the previous financial year
the work test exemption hasn't been used in a previous financial year.

content_blocks.text: Before 1 July 2020 , for members that were between 65 to 75 years old, you could only accept other contributions if they met the work test or the work test exemption.
Members 75 years old or older
You generally can't accept other contributions for these members. However:

items: super co-contributions and employer contributions that relate to a valid contribution period for the member can be accepted at any time
you can accept downsizer contributions if you have the member's TFN.

content_blocks.text: In specie or non-monetary asset contributions
'In specie' contributions are contributions to your fund in the form of a non-monetary asset.
Generally, you must not intentionally acquire assets (including in-specie) from related parties of your fund. However, there are some exceptions to the rule, including:

items: listed shares and other securities
business real property (land and buildings used wholly and exclusively in a business).

content_blocks.text: Employee share schemes

An employee share scheme (ESS) is a program where employers provide employees (including current, past or prospective employees and their associates) with interests in the company. This includes shares, stapled securities and rights to acquire them (including options) as part of their remuneration package. These interests are often offered at a discount to market value .
SMSF trustees are considered associates of fund members, so specific tax rules apply when you acquire ESS related assets from a member.
For more information see TR 2010/1 Income tax: superannuation contributions .
Classifying contributions to an SMSF
You must classify contributions from employee share schemes accurately to meet compliance requirements.

When a member nominates their SMSF to receive shares or share options under an ESS, this typically results in a personal contribution , as the member surrenders or exercises rights personally and nominates the SMSF to receive the asset.
If your SMSF buys an asset for less than market value, the fund receives a super contribution equal to the price difference.
When a member transfers shares or options instead of cash, they make an in-specie contribution.
Each classification:

items: determines whether the fund can accept a contribution under SMSF rules
guides how you report the contribution.

content_blocks.text: Restrictions on SMSFs accepting contributions under an ESS

SMSFs are restricted from acquiring assets from a related party, including a member. If a member nominates you to receive shares or options under an ESS, this results in you acquiring these assets from the member.
For more information, see:

items: Returning contributions

Self-Managed Superannuation Funds Ruling SMSFR 2010/1 Self-Managed Superannuation Funds: the application of section 66(1) of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 to the acquisition of an asset by a self-managed superannuation fund from a related party.

content_text:
## Contributions you can accept
As a self-managed super fund (SMSF) trustee, you can only accept allowable contributions. For a contribution to be allowable:
- it must be an accepted type of contribution, such as mandated employer contributions other contributions in-specie contributions
- the member must meet age restrictions
- member contributions can only be accepted if the SMSF also has the member's tax file number (TFN) .
### When you accept a contribution
When you accept a contribution you must:
- properly document the contribution, including the amount, type and breakdown of components
- allocate it to the member’s account within 28 days of the end of the month in which you received them
- accurately report contributions made to your fund during the financial year in your SMSF annual return.
### Member's tax file number
When a member joins your fund, you should ask for their TFN and give it to us. You can do this when you register the fund or when a new member joins.
A member is not required by law to give their TFN. However, if they don't:
- your fund can't accept member contributions for them, such as personal and eligible spouse contributions
- your fund will have to pay additional tax on their mandated employer contributions
- there may be administrative delays if we can't identify the member from the other information you provided
- the member may not be able to receive super co-contributions .
If the member hasn’t provided their TFN and you’ve accepted member contributions for them, you’ll need to return the contribution within 30 days of becoming aware of the contribution. However, if they provide their TFN within 30 days of receiving the contribution, you don’t have to return the amount.
If you receive employer contributions on behalf of a member and you pay additional income tax because you did not have your member’s TFN, you may be able to claim a tax offset in a later financial year if the member later gives you their TFN.
## Mandated employer contributions
Mandated employer contributions are contributions made by an employer under a law or industrial agreement for the benefit of a fund member. They include super guarantee contributions.
You can accept mandated employer contributions for members at any time, regardless of their age or the number of hours they’re working.
If your SMSF receives contributions from employers (other than related-party employers), your SMSF will need an electronic service address (ESA).
## Other contributions
Other contributions are not required by law as well as Government contributions. They include:
- contributions made by employers over and above their super guarantee or award obligations (such as salary sacrifice contributions)
- member contributions – these are contributions made by or on behalf of a member, such as personal contributions salary sacrifice contributions eligible proceeds from main residence disposal ( downsizer super contributions ) government super co-contributions paid parental leave superannuation contributions eligible spouse contributions contributions made by a third party, such as an insurer
- re-contributing COVID-19 early release super withdrawals .
### When you can accept other contributions
The contributions you are able to accept are affected by the member's age.
#### Members under 75 years old
The rules for members aged under 75 years old have changed over time.
From 1 July 2022 , you can accept all types of other contributions. This includes downsizer super contributions where the member has reached eligible age for the downsizer scheme .
For a member turning 75 years old, contributions must be received no later than 28 days after the end of the month they turn 75.
Between 1 July 2020 and 30 June 2022 , you could accept all types of other contributions for members under 67 years old. If the member was between 67 and 75 years old, they needed to satisfy the work test or the work test exemption before you could accept other contributions.
To satisfy the work test the member must have been gainfully employed for at least 40 hours during a consecutive 30-day period. Where members don't meet the work test, other contributions can be accepted for an extra 12 months where:
- they satisfied the work test in the financial year before the contribution was made
- their total super balance was less than $300,000 at the end of the previous financial year
- the work test exemption hasn't been used in a previous financial year.
Before 1 July 2020 , for members that were between 65 to 75 years old, you could only accept other contributions if they met the work test or the work test exemption.
#### Members 75 years old or older
You generally can't accept other contributions for these members. However:
- super co-contributions and employer contributions that relate to a valid contribution period for the member can be accepted at any time
- you can accept downsizer contributions if you have the member's TFN.
## In specie or non-monetary asset contributions
'In specie' contributions are contributions to your fund in the form of a non-monetary asset.
Generally, you must not intentionally acquire assets (including in-specie) from related parties of your fund. However, there are some exceptions to the rule, including:
- listed shares and other securities
- business real property (land and buildings used wholly and exclusively in a business).
### Employee share schemes
An employee share scheme (ESS) is a program where employers provide employees (including current, past or prospective employees and their associates) with interests in the company. This includes shares, stapled securities and rights to acquire them (including options) as part of their remuneration package. These interests are often offered at a discount to market value .
SMSF trustees are considered associates of fund members, so specific tax rules apply when you acquire ESS related assets from a member.
For more information see TR 2010/1 Income tax: superannuation contributions .
#### Classifying contributions to an SMSF
You must classify contributions from employee share schemes accurately to meet compliance requirements.
When a member nominates their SMSF to receive shares or share options under an ESS, this typically results in a personal contribution , as the member surrenders or exercises rights personally and nominates the SMSF to receive the asset.
If your SMSF buys an asset for less than market value, the fund receives a super contribution equal to the price difference.
When a member transfers shares or options instead of cash, they make an in-specie contribution.
Each classification:
- determines whether the fund can accept a contribution under SMSF rules
- guides how you report the contribution.
#### Restrictions on SMSFs accepting contributions under an ESS
SMSFs are restricted from acquiring assets from a related party, including a member. If a member nominates you to receive shares or options under an ESS, this results in you acquiring these assets from the member.
For more information, see:
- Returning contributions
- Self-Managed Superannuation Funds Ruling SMSFR 2010/1 Self-Managed Superannuation Funds: the application of section 66(1) of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 to the acquisition of an asset by a self-managed superannuation fund from a related party.
source_description: Understand the rules for what contributions you can accept as trustee of a self-managed super fund (SMSF).
